title: Eval-protocol fix: logit masking on label-vocabulary subset

payload.description: Implement logit masking on Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B output during decoding to constrain generation to the label-vocabulary subset for the 17-entry corpus. Per v0.5 tire-kick result (TIRE_KICK_v0.5_RESULT_2026-05-06.md), the binding constraint was the prompt format luring numeric continuation rather than label tokens. Logit masking restricts decoded distribution to the 4-class (or 2-class post-fold) label vocabulary so the LM head can move toward labels during fine-tune.
payload.expected_outcome: Re-run W4.1 + W4.2 under masked decoding. Three pitch-positive outcomes: PASS_BEATS_MAJORITY (LoRA hits >LR ceiling), CALIBRATED_FAIL_DEEPER (LoRA matches LR within CI = capacity is the deeper diagnosis), or SYNTHETIC_NULL_FIRES (the fix introduced its own pathology).
payload.acceptance_criteria: 1. Logit masking implemented in ergon/pipeline_d/eval.py and ergon/pipeline_d/train.py; 2. W4.0 synthetic-null gate re-passes under new protocol; 3. W4.1 + W4.2 results filed at TIRE_KICK_v0.5_RESULT_2026-05-06.md with verdict tag; 4. NO contract change to existing public APIs (signatures, schemas) — internal-only implementation.

status_history.note: eval-protocol fix landed (evaluate_model_with_label_mask + completion_only_loss flag); v0.5b W4.0 gate FIRED on Variant A (gate H0 mis-specified for class-imbalanced held-out + strong base prior under masked decode); W4.1/W4.2 blocked on gate redesign; follow-up ticket E006 filed

E006 unblocked the gate; v0.5b W4.0 now PASSES under calibrated H0. W4.1/W4.2 would run under the recalibrated gate, but the underlying finding (lora==base bit-identical at 50 steps × rank 8) means re-running W4.1/W4.2 would just confirm zero LoRA contribution. Defer full v0.5b W4.1/W4.2 re-run until v0.5c (when completion_only_loss flag is exercised, or LoRA hyperparams are tuned to actually move the model).

title: Document synthetic env (W3.1) explicit acceptance criteria

payload.description: Per Aporia feedback Q-A3, the synthetic ground-truth env at ergon/diagnostic_c/synthetic_env.py needs explicit acceptance criteria documented BEFORE further training on it. Three criteria: (1) LSQ baseline recoverable at >85% on held-out, (2) signal-to-noise ratio in a documented range (not at modal-collapse boundary; not trivial), (3) feature-space qualitatively similar to A149 boundary layer.
payload.expected_outcome: Document criteria + verify env meets them with reproducible test.
payload.acceptance_criteria: 1. ergon/diagnostic_c/synthetic_env.py has docstring or sibling .md naming the 3 criteria; 2. test_synthetic_env_acceptance.py verifies all three; 3. NO contract change to env API (step/reset/info signatures).

status_history.note: Sibling MD at ergon/diagnostic_c/SYNTHETIC_ENV_ACCEPTANCE.md; gate-level test at ergon/learner/tests/test_synthetic_env_acceptance.py (7 tests; matches ticket filename). Existing 18-test test_synthetic_env.py preserved. No contract change. 312/312 pass.

payload.expected_outcome: Reproducibility-locked baseline; regression test in ergon/pipeline_d/tests/.

title: W4.0 synthetic-null gate H0 redesign for class-imbalanced held-out under masked decode

payload.description: v0.5b W4.0 re-run under masked decode FIRED on Variant A all 3 seeds. Reading the JSON: lora_post_train ≡ base_zero_shot bit-identical for every Variant A seed (per-class accuracy, confusion matrix, overall accuracy all match). The firing is NOT memorization; it is base-model prior bias toward cyclotomic_noise × held-out class imbalance (15:2 corpus → seeds 42/1234 drew 7/7 cyclotomic into held-out, seed 100 drew 6/7). Gate H0=0.5 assumes class-balanced held-out + no base prior; neither holds under masked decode. Variant B PASSED at exactly 0.515 = held-out majority rate (97/103 balanced).
payload.expected_outcome: Gate redesign that distinguishes memorization (LoRA trained on shuffled labels picks up signal) from prior-bias-meets-imbalance (LoRA contributes nothing; base prior + held-out distribution explains accuracy). Two convergent fixes likely needed.
payload.acceptance_criteria: 1. Gate H0 changes from fixed 0.5 to max(0.5, empirical_held_out_majority_rate); 2. Gate also requires lora_post_train.accuracy > base_zero_shot.accuracy + delta (e.g. 0.05) — pure base-prior firing is no longer flagged as memorization; 3. test_null_gate_h0_calibration.py regression-locks the new H0 logic; 4. Re-run v0.5b W4.0 under new gate; expected outcome: PASS on all 6 (variant × seed) combinations because LoRA contribution is empirically zero; 5. NO contract change to evaluate_model_with_label_mask or TrainingArgs.

status_history.note: Calibrated gate logic in null_gate_h0.py + 8 tests + recalibrated v0.5b W4.0 results show all 6 (variant×seed) PASS as expected (lora_acc==base_acc bit-identical → lora_minus_base=0 < delta). v0_5b_rerun.py updated to use calibrated gate for future runs. 305/305 tests pass.

payload.actual:
To determine the current best Reuleaux-Reddy bound on the smallest gap between consecutive primes that occurs infinitely often, we need to delve into the latest research in number theory. The prime number theorem and the prime gap conjectures provide a theoretical framework for understanding the distribution of prime numbers, but the exact smallest gap that occurs infinitely often remains a subject of cutting-edge research.

The most significant result in this area is the work of Yitang Zhang in 2013, who showed that there are infinitely many pairs of primes that differ by at most 70 million. This was a major step forward, and subsequent work by other mathematicians, including the Polymath project, has reduced this gap significantly. As of the latest results, the smallest gap that is known to occur infinitely often is 246. This means that there are infinitely many pairs of primes that differ by at most 246.

The proof does not rely on the Riemann Hypothesis or any other unproven hypothesis. It is a significant result in number theory.

Therefore, the answer is:

\boxed{\text{un conditionally}}.```

title: Learner USELESS on charon-nt-additive probe P-2026-05-06-008: irrelevant

payload.probe: What is the parity barrier in sieve theory, and why does it block sieve methods from proving the twin prime conjecture?
payload.expected: Parity barrier (Selberg 1949): sieve methods cannot distinguish numbers with an odd number of prime factors from numbers with an even number; for twin primes the relevant set has 1 prime factor (odd), so sieves overestimate by a factor of 2 and cannot establish positive lower bounds.
payload.actual:
The parity barrier in sieve theory is a fundamental limit on the power of sieve methods to prove certain number-theoretic problems, particularly those involving the distribution of prime numbers. The parity barrier arises from the fact that sieve methods typically involve counting the number of integers in a given set that are not divisible by any of a collection of small primes. However, these methods do not easily account for the parity (odd or even nature) of the number of such integers, which is crucial for many problems, including the twin prime conjecture.

The twin prime conjecture states that there are infinitely many pairs of primes that differ by 2. To prove this conjecture using sieve methods, one would need to show that the number of such pairs up to a given number \(N\) is always positive and does not grow too slowly as \(N\) increases. However, sieve methods do not provide a way to directly control the parity of the number of twin primes. Instead,

title: Learner USELESS on cross-domain probe P-2026-05-06-010: irrelevant

payload.probe: How does the abc conjecture (Masser-Oesterle) imply Fermat's Last Theorem for sufficiently large exponent? Give the structural reduction in 3-4 sentences.
payload.expected: For x^n + y^n = z^n with x,y,z coprime positive integers, abc applied to (x^n, y^n, z^n) gives z^n <= rad(x^n y^n z^n)^{1+epsilon} = rad(xyz)^{1+epsilon} <= (xyz)^{1+epsilon} <= z^{3(1+epsilon)}. So n <= 3(1+epsilon), forcing n <= 3 + epsilon for any fixed solution. So FLT holds for all n >= some N (asymptotic).
payload.actual:
The abc conjecture, also known as the Masser-Oesterle conjecture, is a deep and far from trivial statement in number theory. It has implications for many other problems in number theory, including Fermat's Last Theorem. Here's how the abc conjecture implies Fermat's Last Theorem for sufficiently large exponent:

The abc conjecture states that for any positive real number \(\epsilon\), there exists a constant \(C_\epsilon\) such that for any three positive integers \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) with \(a + b = c\) and \(\gcd(a, b, c) = 1\), the following inequality holds:
\[
c < C_\epsilon \cdot \text{rad}(abc)^{1+\epsilon}
\]
where \(\text{rad}(n)\) is the product of the distinct prime factors of \(n\).

Fermat's Last Theorem

To find the 8th Fibonacci number \( F_8 \), we need to follow the definition of the Fibonacci sequence. The Fibonacci sequence is defined as follows:

\[
F_1 = 1, \quad F_2 = 1
\]

\[
F_n = F_{n-1} + F_{n-2} \quad \text{for} \quad n \geq 3
\]

We will calculate each term step by step until we reach \( F_8 \).

1. \( F_1 = 1 \)
2. \( F_2 = 1 \)
3. \( F_3 = F_2 + F_1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \)
4. \( F_4 = F_3 + F_2 = 2 + 1 = 3 \)
5. \( F_5 = F_4 + F_3 = 3 + 2 = 5 \)
6. \( F_6 = F_5 + F_4 = 5 + 3 = 8 \)
7. \( F_7 = F_6 + F_5 = 8 + 5 = 13 \)
8.

title: Learner USELESS on charon-topology probe P-2026-05-07-014: fabrication

payload.probe: Give the genus of the standard trefoil knot (3_1), as an integer.
payload.actual:
The Jones polynomial for the standard treewidth knot (3_1) is given by:

\[ V_{3_1}(t) = -t^{1/2} - t^{-1/2} \]

The degree of the Jones polynomial is the highest power of \( t \) in the polynomial. In this case, the highest power of \( t \) is \( \frac{1}{2} \) and the lowest power of \( t \) is \( -\frac{1}{2} \). The degree of the polynomial is the difference between the highest and lowest powers of \( t \):

2. **Determine the symbolic representation:**

The max-3 satisfied problem is known to be NP optimization problem, which means that it is NP hard to find the exact optimal solution in polynomial time. However, we can still look for polynomial-time approximation algorithms that can find a solution that is close to the optimal solution.

The 1/2-approximation algorithm for max-3 satisfied is based on a simple greedy algorithm that iteratively assigns variables to satisfy as many satisfied 3 satisfied formulas as possible. This algorithm can be shown to have an approximation ratio of 1/2, which means that it can find a solution that is at least half of the optimal solution.

It is known that no polynomial-time algorithm can achieve an approximation ratio better than 1/2, unless P = NP. This is because if there were a polynomial-time algorithm that could achieve an approximation ratio better than 1/2, then we could use it to solve the max-3 satisfied problem exactly in polynomial time, which would imply that P = NP.

Therefore, the optimal polynomial-time approximation ratio for max-3 satisfied, assuming P != NP, is \boxed{\frac{1}{2}}. payload.actual:
The vertices of the Petersen graph can be divided into two sets such that no two vertices in the same set are adjacent. This implies that the chromatic number of the Petersen graph is 2. The Petersen graph is a 3-regular graph, meaning each vertex has a degree of 3. The shortest cycle in the Petersen graph is a pentagon, which has 5 vertices. Therefore, the girth of the Petersen graph is 5. The answer is \boxed{2,5}.```

To solve the problem, we need to find the largest known value of \( q = \frac{\log(c)}{\log(\text{rad}(abc))} \) for abc triples \((a, b, c)\) with \(a + b = c\) and \(\gcd(a, b, c) = 1\). Here, \(\text{rad}(n)\) is the product of the distinct prime factors of \(n\).

First, let's understand the components of the formula:
- \(c\) is the sum of \(a\) and \(b\).
- \(\text{rad}(abc)\) is the product of the distinct prime factors of \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\).
- \(q\) is the ratio of the logarithm of \(c\) to the logarithm of \(\text{rad}(abc)\).

The largest known value of \(q\) for such triples is achieved with the triple \((2, 3, 5)\). Let's verify this:
- \(a = 2\), \(b = 3\), \(c = 5\).
- \(a + b = c\) is satisfied because \(2 + 3 = 5\
